1: A type: Joshua called the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh — the completion of the Transjordanian tribes’ service commitment. They had promised to help the western tribes conquer Canaan before receiving their own inheritance (Joshua 1:12-15); now Joshua formally releases them. The dismissal pattern — faithful service completed, formal release given, return to inheritance — is the type of the servant’s completion of commissioned work. Christ’s words at the cross “It is finished” (tetelestai, John 19:30) are the ultimate completion announcement: the service-commitment to the Father’s mission accomplished, the return to glory (John 17:4-5) secured.
2: A direct revelation (through Joshua’s words): “You have kept all that Moses the servant of YHWH commanded you and have obeyed my voice in all that I have commanded you.” The commendation of the eastern tribes’ faithful service is the form of the covenant verdict: “well done, good and faithful servant” (Matthew 25:21). Joshua’s words here anticipate Christ’s final judgment-commendation — the covenant leader’s verdict on the covenant community’s faithfulness. The Transjordanian tribes receive the commendation before crossing to their inheritance, as believers will receive the commendation at the judgment before entering the final inheritance.
3: A type: “You have not forsaken your brothers these many days, down to this day, but have been careful to keep the charge of YHWH your God.” The faithfulness through the long campaign — the “many days” of the entire conquest — is the evidence Joshua cites. Covenantal faithfulness is demonstrated through duration, not merely intensity: not the dramatic initial commitment but the sustained presence through the years of conflict. Hebrews 3:14: “we have come to share in Christ, if indeed we hold our original confidence firm to the end.” The holding firm through the many days is the form of the covenant faithfulness that Joshua commends.
4: A direct revelation (through Joshua): “And now YHWH your God has given rest to your brothers, as he promised them. Therefore turn and go to your tents in the land where your possession lies, which Moses the servant of YHWH gave you beyond the Jordan.” The dismissal to the inheritance is the completion of service leading to Sabbath: work done, rest entered. The rest-gift given to all Israel now allows the service-workers to rest in their own inheritance. Philippians 4:7: “the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus” — the peace after faithful service is YHWH’s gift, not the warrior’s achievement.
5: A direct revelation (Joshua’s charge): “Only be very careful to observe the commandment and the law that Moses the servant of YHWH commanded you, to love YHWH your God, and to walk in all his ways and to keep his commandments and to cling to him and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul.” This verse contains the Shema applied to covenant life in the land: the full-devotion formula that Jesus names as the greatest commandment (Matthew 22:37-38: “Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind — this is the great and first commandment”). Joshua’s charge at the tribal dismissal is a rehearsal of the Shema; Jesus’s summary of the law is its fulfillment and distillation.
6: A type: Joshua blessed them and sent them away, and they went to their tents. The blessing before departure — the covenant leader’s benediction that releases the community to their inheritance — is the priestly blessing function. Numbers 6:24-26: “YHWH bless you and keep you; YHWH make his face shine on you and be gracious to you” — the Aaronite blessing given through the priests. Christ’s ascension blessing over his disciples (Luke 24:50-51: “lifting up his hands he blessed them. While he blessed them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven”) is the definitive priestly blessing at the moment of departure — the ascending leader’s benediction releasing his people to their mission.
7: A type: to the half-tribe of Manasseh Moses had given a portion in Bashan, and to the other half Joshua had given a portion among their brothers west of the Jordan. The split inheritance of Manasseh — half east, half west of the Jordan — creates a tribe whose unity spans both sides of the covenant boundary. The divided-but-united Manasseh is the type of the church’s unity across cultural boundaries: neither the Transjordanian nor the Cisjordanian half is the “real” Manasseh — both halves together constitute the tribe. Ephesians 2:14: “he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility.”
8: A type: Joshua blessed the eastern tribes and sent them home with great wealth — livestock, silver, gold, bronze, iron, and a great quantity of clothing. The blessing includes sharing the plunder of the conquest — the eastern tribes, though they received their inheritance east of the Jordan, share in the wealth of the western campaign. Second Corinthians Joshua 8:9: “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich” — the pattern of wealth-sharing through covenant service is the type of Christ’s kenosis producing abundance for his people. The eastern tribes go home enriched by the western conquest they helped accomplish.
9: A type: the Reubenites and Gadites and the half-tribe of Manasseh left the people of Israel at Shiloh and went to the land of Gilead. Shiloh, the central sanctuary site, is the last shared covenant-space before the separation. The departure from Shiloh — the place of YHWH’s tabernacle — marks the transition from shared worship-space to separated territorial existence. The theology of separation from the visible sanctuary will become urgent in the altar-controversy (Joshua 22:10-34): can YHWH’s presence be claimed apart from the official sanctuary? John 4:21-24 resolves this definitively: neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem — the true worshipers worship in spirit and truth, wherever they are.
10: A type: at the Jordan, in the land of Canaan, the Reubenites and Gadites and half-tribe of Manasseh built there an altar — a large and impressive altar. The altar at the Jordan is not a sacrifice-altar but a witness-monument — though the western tribes misunderstand it as apostasy and prepare for civil war. The misunderstanding of an act of covenant commitment as an act of rebellion is the hermeneutical crisis that the chapter resolves. The cross is the ultimate hermeneutical crisis: what appeared to be God’s judgment and defeat of the righteous one was YHWH’s supreme act of covenant faithfulness. The Jordan altar is “impressive” (gadol lemar'eh); the cross is an instrument of execution — both look like the wrong thing until properly interpreted.
11: A type: the people of Israel heard, “Behold, the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh have built the altar at the frontier of the land of Canaan, at the Jordan.” The report reaches the whole community as a shock — an altar outside the approved sanctuary location seemed to violate the deuteronomic command of centralized worship (Deuteronomy 12:5-14). The community’s immediate alarm reflects the same principle as Galatians 1:6-9: a different gospel is no gospel at all, and the alarm at theological deviation is itself a sign of covenantal seriousness. The western tribes’ alarm is not legalism but pastoral care for covenant integrity.
12: A type: the whole assembly of Israel gathered at Shiloh to go up to war against them. The mobilization for civil war at Shiloh — the very place from which the eastern tribes just received their blessing — shows how quickly covenant misunderstanding can generate catastrophic conflict. The prevention of civil war through investigation and dialogue (Phinehas’s delegation) is the model of church conflict resolution: 1 Corinthians 6:1-6 counsels settling disputes within the community rather than going to war (or to outside courts); Matthew 18:15-17 prescribes the progressive confrontation that Phinehas’s embassy enacts here.
13: A type: the people of Israel sent Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest to the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh in the land of Gilead. Phinehas, who turned away YHWH’s wrath through his zealous action at Peor (Numbers 25:7-13), is now sent as the covenant mediator/investigator. The man whose zeal prevented divine wrath is sent as the agent of covenant discernment — the same person who was the avenging priest becomes the reconciling ambassador. Christ who bears the full measure of divine judgment (Colossians 1:20: “making peace by the blood of his cross”) is simultaneously the agent of reconciliation sent to those who appeared to be covenant-breakers.
14: A type: ten chiefs from the tribes of Israel — one per tribe — were sent with Phinehas. The representative-per-tribe structure of the embassy ensures that the confrontation is a covenant-community act, not a unilateral accusation. The ten chiefs represent the entire western covenant community’s concern, making their conclusion binding on all Israel. The principle of communal discernment — sending representatives of the whole body — is the church-council principle enacted in Acts 15 at Jerusalem, where representatives of the whole church investigate the Gentile-circumcision question and reach a binding conclusion.
15: A type: Phinehas and the ten chiefs came to the land of Gilead and spoke to the Reubenites and Gadites and half-tribe of Manasseh and said to them. The direct confrontation — face to face, naming the concern explicitly — is the form of covenant love: “you sinned against YHWH” is stated directly, not whispered. Matthew 18:15: “if your brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault, between you and him alone.” Phinehas’s embassy is the covenant-community form of this direct confrontation, scaled to a tribal level but following the same principle of loving directness.
16: A type: the accusation — “What is this breach of faith (ma'al) you have committed against the God of Israel?” The term ma'al (breach of faith in sacred things) is the technical term for sacrilege that appeared in Achan’s sin (Joshua 7:1). The western tribes are applying the most serious covenantal charge possible — the same charge that brought disaster at Ai. The seriousness of the accusation reflects the seriousness of covenant integrity: the community cannot afford to treat a ma'al lightly, because YHWH’s presence with the whole community depends on covenant purity.
17: A type: is the iniquity of Peor too little for us, from which we have not cleansed ourselves even to this day, and there came a plague upon the congregation of YHWH? The warning by analogy — Peor caused a plague on all Israel — shows that individual or sub-group sin affects the whole covenant body. The Peor incident (Num 25: sexual immorality with Moabite women) is the paradigm of how sin in one part of the covenant community generates judgment on the whole. First Corinthians Joshua 5:6: “your boasting is not good. Do you not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump?” — the same logic as the Peor warning: corporate accountability requires corporate vigilance.
18: A type: and you must turn away today from following YHWH, and tomorrow he will be angry with the whole congregation of Israel. The temporal logic — your act today brings YHWH’s anger on all tomorrow — is the covenant solidarity principle at maximum clarity. What any part of the covenant body does affects the whole body’s standing before the covenant suzerain. Ephesians 4:15-16: “speaking the truth in love, we are to grow up in every way into him who is the head... the whole body, joined and held together by every joint with which it is equipped... makes the body grow” — the health of each part affects the whole, as the sin of each part endangers the whole.
19: A type: if your land is unclean, cross over to the LORD’s land where his tabernacle stands and take a possession among us. The invitation to cross over — to physically relocate to the land of YHWH’s sanctuary presence rather than remain in separate territory — is the maximum offer of covenant incorporation. Hebrews 10:19-22: “we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus... let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith.” The geographic crossing-over to the sanctuary that the western tribes offered the eastern tribes is surpassed by Christ’s opening of the way into the Most Holy Place — the true crossing-over that abolishes all geographic distance from the divine presence.
20: A type: did not Achan son of Zerah commit ma'al regarding the devoted things, and wrath fell upon all Israel? The Achan analogy confirms the western tribes’ logic: one person’s sacrilege brought judgment on all Israel, and he did not perish alone in his sin. The corporate liability principle — one sinning causes all to suffer — is the background for understanding Christ’s substitutionary atonement: just as Adam’s sin brought death to all (Romans 5:12: “death spread to all men because all sinned”), so Christ’s obedience brings life to all who are in him (Romans 5:18-19). The Achan-on-all-Israel type is the negative form of the Adam-on-all-humanity type that Christ reverses.
21: A type: the eastern tribes’ defense — invoking the divine triple name as witness: “El Elohim YHWH! El Elohim YHWH! He knows, and let Israel itself know.” The triple divine-name invocation as solemn oath-formula is the highest possible covenant witness. The accumulation of divine names — El (the mighty one), Elohim (God), YHWH (the covenant name) — invokes the full divine character as witness to the oath’s truthfulness. Hebrews 6:13: “when God made a promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself.” The divine self-witness in the triple name is the covenant-oath form that anticipates the Father’s testimony to the Son (John 5:37: “the Father who sent me has himself borne witness about me”).
22: A type: if it was in rebellion or in breach of faith against YHWH — do not spare us today. The eastern tribes submit to the western community’s judgment: if we are guilty, execute us. The willingness to accept the full covenant penalty if proven guilty is the form of covenant integrity. Christ’s submission to the Father’s judgment — “not my will, but yours, be done” (Luke 22:42) — is the perfect form of this submission. He who was not guilty submitted to the full penalty on behalf of those who were: the ultimate inversion of the eastern tribes’ conditional submission (they said “punish us if guilty”; Christ said “I will receive the punishment for the guilty”).
23: A type: if we built the altar to turn away from following YHWH — let YHWH himself require it. The self-imprecation with YHWH as the executor of the penalty is the covenant-oath’s ultimate form: appealing to YHWH himself as both judge and executioner if they lie. The logic anticipates Hebrews 10:28-31: the severity of covenant judgment under the old covenant was real; how much more severe the judgment for trampling the Son of God underfoot (v29). The eastern tribes’ self-imprecation with YHWH as judge is the shadow of the “it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” (Hebrews 10:31).
24: A type: the explanation — they built the altar because they feared future generations would be told “what have you to do with YHWH the God of Israel? YHWH has made the Jordan a boundary between us and you.” The fear that geographic separation would become covenantal separation drives the altar’s construction. The anxiety about being cut off from the covenant community by a physical boundary is the pastoral heart of the eastern tribes’ action. Christ’s own prayer (John 17:11: “Holy Father, keep them in your name that you have given me, that they may be one, even as we are one”) addresses the same fear: that the physical separation of death and distance might break the unity of his community.
25: A type: “YHWH has made the Jordan a boundary between us and you, you people of Reuben and Gad; you have no portion in YHWH” — the feared future exclusion. The anxiety that the Jordan’s boundary would create a theological second-class — people with “no portion in YHWH” — is the covenant-exclusion fear that the altar addresses. In the NT, the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile created exactly this anxiety (Ephesians 2:12: Gentiles were “alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world”). Christ’s reconciling work eliminates the dividing boundary: no Jordan-boundary, no Gentile-exclusion, in the one body.
26: A type: “Therefore we said, ‘Let us now build an altar’ — not for burnt offering nor for sacrifice, but to be a witness between us and you.” The witness-altar is a monument to covenant unity, not a rival place of sacrifice. The distinction between the sacrificial altar (where YHWH’s atonement is enacted) and the witness monument (that testifies to covenant belonging) is critical. There is one altar of sacrifice: Christ’s cross (Hebrews 10:10-14: “by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all”). All subsequent monuments and memorials — including the Lord’s Supper — are witness-monuments to the one sacrifice, not supplements to it.
27: A type: the altar as witness that they perform the service of YHWH before him with burnt offerings and sacrifices — lest future generations say they have no portion. The witness function of the altar is explicitly forward-looking: it is for future generations, not present benefit. The Lord’s Supper is the NT counterpart — “do this in remembrance of me... until he comes” (1 Corinthians 11:24-26) — a forward-looking witness monument that declares the covenant meal’s validity for every future generation until Christ returns. The Jordan altar anticipates the Supper’s trans-generational witness function.
28: A type: “if they say this to us in the future, we shall reply ‘Behold the copy of the altar of YHWH which our fathers made.’” The altar as the physical argument against future exclusion — a copy (tabnit) of the true altar, a witness monument — is the type of every covenant memorial that grounds present identity in past act. Baptism functions similarly in the NT: the water memorial declares the one baptism into Christ’s death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4) as the ground of present covenant identity. The copy-altar points back to the original act; baptism points back to the defining act of death and resurrection.
29: A type: “Far be it from us (chalilah lanu) that we should rebel against YHWH and turn away this day from following YHWH by building an altar for burnt offerings, grain offerings, or sacrifices, other than the altar of YHWH our God that stands before his tabernacle.” The eastern tribes’ strongest possible rejection of any rival sacrifice-altar affirms the one sanctuary principle. There is one altar of atonement: the tabernacle/temple altar, to which all legitimate sacrifice belongs. In the NT, there is one atonement: the cross of Christ. Hebrews 13:10: “we have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat” — Christ’s sacrifice is the one altar that the entire new covenant community shares.
30: A type: when Phinehas and the chiefs of the congregation heard the words of the Reubenites and Gadites and Manassites, it was good in their eyes. The resolution — the confrontation leads to understanding, which produces peace — is the form of Matthew 18:15: “if he listens to you, you have gained your brother.” The process of direct confrontation, full explanation, and public vindication is the covenant community’s conflict-resolution mechanism working as YHWH designed it. The near-civil-war ends not with conquest but with comprehension — a more complete victory than military force could achieve.
31: A type: Phinehas said, “Today we know that YHWH is in our midst, because you have not committed this breach of faith against YHWH. Now you have delivered the people of Israel from the hand of YHWH.” The priest’s declaration — YHWH is in our midst because you acted faithfully — makes covenant integrity the condition of divine presence. The obverse is equally true: covenant faithfulness preserves divine presence, while ma'al drives it away. This principle reaches its fulfillment in Christ’s promise: “where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them” (Matthew 18:20) — the community’s faithful covenant practice becomes the locus of divine presence.
32: A type: Phinehas and the chiefs returned from the land of Gilead to the land of Canaan and reported to the people of Israel. The resolution’s public announcement — the ambassador’s return and report — completes the covenant process: confrontation, investigation, resolution, public reporting. The report is the form of 2 Corinthians 5:20 (“we are ambassadors for Christ, God making his appeal through us”) — the ambassador who goes, investigates, reconciles, and returns with news of peace. Phinehas’s embassy is one of the OT’s clearest models of the gospel-ambassador role.
33: A type: the people of Israel were pleased, and blessed God and spoke no more of making war against them, and did not go up to destroy the land. The averted civil war — the near-catastrophe resolved by faithful process — leaves the community at peace. The blessing of God that the community offers after the resolution is the covenantal doxology: praise is the right response to preserved unity. Romans 15:5-6: “May the God of endurance and encouragement grant you to live in such harmony with one another... so that together you may with one voice glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.”
34: A type: the Reubenites and Gadites called the altar Witness (Ed), “for it is a witness between us that YHWH is God.” The altar named “Witness” becomes a permanent physical declaration: YHWH is God, and this stone testifies to the covenant unity of the separated communities. Revelation 3:14 names Christ as “the faithful and true witness (martus)” — the ultimate Witness who testifies that YHWH is God and that his covenant people are one. The Jordan altar’s name is a type: the true Ed/Witness between God and his people, between the separated communities of east and west, Jew and Gentile, is Christ himself.
